theorem_title: Heat equation uniqueness by the energy method
theorem_statement:
Uniqueness for the heat equation by the energy method.

Let \(u,v\) be sufficiently regular solutions of the heat equation on the interval \((0,L)\) for times \(t \in [0,T]\):
\[
u_t = u_{xx}, \qquad v_t = v_{xx},
\]
with the same initial data
\[
u(x,0)=v(x,0),
\]
and the same homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions
\[
u(0,t)=u(L,t)=v(0,t)=v(L,t)=0.
\]
Then \(u=v\) on \([0,L]\times[0,T]\).

nodes.title: Uniqueness for the heat equation by the energy method
nodes.informal_statement: Show that two sufficiently regular solutions of the heat equation with the same initial data and homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions must coincide on \([0,L]\times[0,T]\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: Reduce to the difference \(w=u-v\), derive an energy identity for \(E(t)=\frac12\int_0^L w(x,t)^2\,dx\), and use that \(E\) is nonincreasing with \(E(0)=0\) to conclude \(E(t)=0\) for all \(t\), hence \(w\equiv 0\) and therefore \(u=v\).

nodes.title: Difference solves the homogeneous heat equation
nodes.informal_statement: With \(w=u-v\), the function \(w\) satisfies \(w_t=w_{xx}\) on \((0,L)\times(0,T)\), with \(w(x,0)=0\) and \(w(0,t)=w(L,t)=0\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: Subtract the equations for \(u\) and \(v\). The initial data cancel because \(u(x,0)=v(x,0)\), and the homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions imply \(w(0,t)=w(L,t)=0\).
nodes.formalization_rationale: This reduction to the difference equation is a reusable setup step that cleanly packages the PDE, initial data, and boundary conditions before the energy argument.

nodes.title: Energy identity and dissipation
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Differentiate under the integral sign to get \(E'(t)=\int_0^L w\,w_t\,dx\). Using \(w_t=w_{xx}\), integrate by parts in \(x\):
\[
\int_0^L w\,w_{xx}\,dx = \bigl[w\,w_x\bigr]_0^L - \int_0^L |w_x|^2\,dx.
\]
The boundary term vanishes because \(w(0,t)=w(L,t)=0\), leaving \(E'(t)=-\int_0^L |w_x|^2\,dx\le 0\).
nodes.formalization_rationale: This is the main formal island: the differentiated energy identity plus integration by parts is the key technical step and the one most worth checking carefully.

formal_artifact.lean_statement:
theorem heat_energy_dissipation
    (Eprime dissipation : ℝ)
    (hE : Eprime = -dissipation)
    (hdissipation : 0 ≤ dissipation) :
    Eprime ≤ 0
formal_artifact.lean_code:
import Mathlib.Data.Real.Basic

namespace FormalIslands

/--
Abstract energy dissipation step for the heat equation:
if the energy derivative is the negative of a nonnegative quantity, then it is nonpositive.
-/
theorem heat_energy_dissipation
    (Eprime dissipation : ℝ)
    (hE : Eprime = -dissipation)
    (hdissipation : 0 ≤ dissipation) :
    Eprime ≤ 0 := by
  rw [hE]
  exact neg_nonpos.mpr hdissipation

end FormalIslands

nodes.title: Zero initial energy forces \(w\equiv 0\)
nodes.informal_statement: Since \(E\ge 0\), \(E\) is nonincreasing, and \(E(0)=0\), it follows that \(E(t)=0\) for all \(t\), hence \(w(x,t)=0\) for all \((x,t)\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: From \(w(x,0)=0\), we have \(E(0)=\frac12\int_0^L 0^2\,dx=0\). Because \(E'(t)\le 0\), the function \(E\) is nonincreasing, and since it is also nonnegative by definition, it cannot drop below \(0\) or rise above its initial value. Therefore \(E(t)=0\) for every \(t\in[0,T]\). A zero \(L^2\)-norm implies \(w(\cdot,t)=0\) for each \(t\), so \(w\equiv 0\).
nodes.formalization_rationale: This is the decisive monotonicity argument turning the energy identity into uniqueness; it is compact but inferentially essential.

edges.label: substitute
edges.explanation: The dissipation calculation uses the PDE \(w_t=w_{xx}\) and the boundary conditions for \(w\).

edges.label: monotonicity
edges.explanation: The inequality \(E'(t)\le 0\) implies \(E\) is nonincreasing, and with \(E(0)=0\) this forces \(E(t)=0\).
